questions.subject: Physics
options: Simple harmonic motion is always oscillatory.
Simple harmonic motion is always periodic.
questions.solution:
Periodic motion is a motion that repeats itself after equal intervals of time. It need not be oscillatory. For example, uniform circular motion is periodic but not oscillatory.

Oscillatory motion is a to and fro motion about a mean position. It need not be periodic. For example, in damped harmonic motion, the amplitude decreases with time, so the motion does not repeat exactly and is not strictly periodic.

Simple harmonic motion (SHM) is a special case of oscillatory motion where the restoring force is directly proportional to the displacement from the mean position. It is always to and fro (oscillatory) and repeats after equal intervals of time (periodic).

Therefore, simple harmonic motion is always oscillatory and always periodic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a simple harmonic motion, which of the following quantities are always equal to zero?
questions.solution:
In simple harmonic motion, the restoring force $\vec{F}$ is proportional to the displacement $\vec{r}$ from the mean position and is directed opposite to it.

$\vec{F} = -k\vec{r}$

By Newton's second law, the acceleration $\vec{a}$ is given by:

$\vec{a} = \dfrac{\vec{F}}{m} = -\omega^2\vec{r}$

Since $\vec{F}$, $\vec{a}$, and $\vec{r}$ are always collinear (either parallel or anti-parallel to each other), the cross product of any two of these vectors is always zero.

$\vec{F} \times \vec{a} = \vec{0}$

$\vec{F} \times \vec{r} = \vec{0}$

$\vec{a} \times \vec{r} = \vec{0}$

The velocity $\vec{v}$ and displacement $\vec{r}$ are collinear in linear SHM, so their dot product $\vec{v} \cdot \vec{r}$ is not always zero (it is zero only at the mean and extreme positions).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During a simple harmonic motion, which of the following quantities will always be negative?
questions.solution:
In simple harmonic motion, the restoring force $\vec{F}$ and acceleration $\vec{a}$ are always directed towards the mean position, while the displacement $\vec{r}$ is directed away from the mean position.

Mathematically, $\vec{F} = -k\vec{r}$ and $\vec{a} = -\omega^2\vec{r}$, where $k$ and $\omega^2$ are positive constants.

Evaluating the given dot products:

$\vec{F} \cdot \vec{a} = (-k\vec{r}) \cdot (-\omega^2\vec{r}) = k\omega^2 r^2 \ge 0$

$\vec{v} \cdot \vec{r}$ can be positive (when the particle is moving away from the mean position) or negative (when moving towards the mean position).

$\vec{F} \cdot \vec{r} = (-k\vec{r}) \cdot \vec{r} = -kr^2 \le 0$

$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{r} = (-\omega^2\vec{r}) \cdot \vec{r} = -\omega^2 r^2 \le 0$

Thus, $\vec{F} \cdot \vec{r}$ and $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{r}$ will always be negative for any non-zero displacement.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The motion executed by a torsional pendulum is classified as
questions.solution:
In a torsional pendulum, the restoring torque $\tau$ is directly proportional to the angular displacement $\theta$ and is directed towards the mean position. This is given by the equation:

$\tau = -C\theta$

where $C$ is the torsional constant of the suspension wire.

This equation is the defining condition for angular simple harmonic motion.

Since the pendulum undergoes to-and-fro motion about a stable equilibrium position, the motion is oscillatory. Furthermore, because this motion repeats itself after a fixed interval of time, it is also periodic.

Therefore, the motion executed by a torsional pendulum is classified as angular simple harmonic, oscillatory, and periodic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is secured to the end of a string and whirled in a vertical circle while the opposite end of the string remains fixed. The motion of the particle is
questions.solution:
When a particle is whirled in a vertical circle, it continuously moves along the circular path, completing revolutions in a single direction.

Since the particle repeats its entire trajectory after a definite interval of time, its motion is periodic.

Because the particle does not move to and fro about a mean equilibrium position, the motion is neither oscillatory nor simple harmonic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a tunnel excavated along a diameter of the earth. A particle is released from a point at a distance $h$ directly above the tunnel. As viewed from the earth, the motion of the particle is
questions.solution:
When the particle is released from a height $h$ directly above the tunnel, it falls towards the center of the earth due to gravity.

Assuming the earth is non-rotating, the initial velocity is zero, and the gravitational force is purely radial. Thus, the particle moves along a straight line passing through the center of the earth.

For $r > R$ (outside the earth), the gravitational force is $F = -\dfrac{GMm}{r^2}$.

For $r < R$ (inside the earth), the gravitational force is $F = -\dfrac{GMmr}{R^3}$.

Since the force is always directed towards the mean position (center of the earth) and the system is conservative, the particle will cross the center, reach a distance $h$ on the opposite side, and return. Hence, the motion is periodic.

However, because the restoring force is not proportional to the displacement for the entire journey (specifically for $r > R$), the motion is not simple harmonic.

Thus, the motion is on a straight line and periodic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a particle undergoes simple harmonic motion, its acceleration is proportional to
options: the distance it has travelled since $t = 0$
its displacement from the mean position
its distance from the mean position
questions.solution:
For a particle executing simple harmonic motion, the restoring force acting on it is directly proportional to its displacement from the mean position and is directed towards the mean position.

$F = -kx$

$ma = -kx$

$a = -\dfrac{k}{m}x = -\omega^2 x$

Thus, the acceleration of the particle is directly proportional to its displacement from the mean position.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle travels in the X-Y plane governed by the equation $\vec{r} = (\vec{i} + 2\vec{j}) A \cos\omega t$. The motion of this particle is
questions.solution:
Given position vector $\vec{r} = (\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}) A \cos\omega t$

The $x$ and $y$ coordinates of the particle are:
$x = A \cos\omega t$
$y = 2A \cos\omega t$

Eliminating $t$, we get:
$y = 2x$
This is the equation of a straight line passing through the origin, so the particle moves on a straight line.

Differentiating $\vec{r}$ with respect to time $t$ to find acceleration:
$\vec{v} = \dfrac{d\vec{r}}{dt} = -(\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}) A \omega \sin\omega t$
$\vec{a} = \dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = -(\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}) A \omega^2 \cos\omega t$
$\vec{a} = -\omega^2 \vec{r}$

Since the acceleration is directly proportional to the negative of the position vector, the motion is simple harmonic.

Every simple harmonic motion is inherently periodic.

Thus, the motion is periodic, on a straight line, and simple harmonic.

questions.subject: Physics
options: the average potential energy over an arbitrary time interval equals the average kinetic energy for that same interval
the potential energy is never equal to the kinetic energy
the average potential energy over a single time period equals the average kinetic energy for this period
the potential energy is continuously equal to the kinetic energy
questions.solution:
In a simple harmonic motion, the displacement and velocity as functions of time are given by $x = A \sin(\omega t)$ and $v = A \omega \cos(\omega t)$.

The instantaneous potential energy is $U = \dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^2 x^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2 \sin^2(\omega t)$.

The instantaneous kinetic energy is $K = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2 \cos^2(\omega t)$.

The average potential energy over one complete time period $T$ is:

$\langle U \rangle = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2 \sin^2(\omega t) dt = \dfrac{1}{4} m \omega^2 A^2$

The average kinetic energy over one complete time period $T$ is:

$\langle K \rangle = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2 \cos^2(\omega t) dt = \dfrac{1}{4} m \omega^2 A^2$

Thus, the average potential energy over a single time period is exactly equal to the average kinetic energy for this period.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a system executing simple harmonic motion,
options: the maximum potential energy is equal to the minimum kinetic energy
the minimum potential energy is equal to the maximum kinetic energy
the maximum potential energy is equal to the maximum kinetic energy
the minimum potential energy is equal to the minimum kinetic energy
questions.solution:
For a system executing simple harmonic motion, the total mechanical energy is conserved.

Let the displacement of the particle from the mean position be $x$ and the amplitude be $A$.

The kinetic energy of the system is given by:

$K = \dfrac{1}{2}m\omega^2(A^2 - x^2)$

Assuming the potential energy to be zero at the mean position, the potential energy is given by:

$U = \dfrac{1}{2}m\omega^2 x^2$

At the mean position ($x = 0$):

$K_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2}m\omega^2 A^2$

$U_{min} = 0$

At the extreme positions ($x = \pm A$):

$K_{min} = 0$

$U_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2}m\omega^2 A^2$

Comparing the maximum and minimum values, we get:

$U_{max} = K_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2}m\omega^2 A^2$

$U_{min} = K_{min} = 0$

Thus, the maximum potential energy is equal to the maximum kinetic energy, and the minimum potential energy is equal to the minimum kinetic energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object is released from rest. A pendulum clock is utilized to measure both the time it takes to fall through a distance $h$ and the speed of the object as it covers this distance. The complete apparatus is subsequently transported to the moon, where the experiment is performed again.
options: The actual times of the fall are equal.
questions.solution:
The actual time of fall is given by $t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{g}}$.

The time period of a pendulum clock is $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}}$.

The measured time is proportional to the number of oscillations the pendulum makes during the fall, which is $n = \dfrac{t}{T} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{L}}$.

Since $n$ is independent of the acceleration due to gravity $g$, the measured times are identical on the Earth and the Moon.

The actual speed of the object after falling a distance $h$ is $v = \sqrt{2gh}$, which depends on $g$. Thus, the actual speeds are different.

The measured speed is calculated using the measured time. Since the measured time and the distance $h$ are identical in both cases, the measured speeds are also identical.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following will experience a change in their time period when transported to the moon?
questions.solution:
The time period of a simple pendulum is given by $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$. Since the acceleration due to gravity $g$ on the moon is different from that on Earth, its time period will change.

The time period of a physical pendulum is given by $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{mgd}}$. This also depends on $g$, so its time period will change.

The time period of a torsional pendulum is $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{C}}$, where $C$ is the torsional constant. It is independent of $g$, so its time period will not change.

The time period of a spring-mass system is $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$, where $k$ is the spring constant. It is independent of $g$, so its time period will not change.

Thus, only the simple pendulum and the physical pendulum will experience a change in their time periods.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle travels in a circular path at a uniform speed. Its motion can be described as
questions.solution:
A particle moving in a circular path at a uniform speed completes one revolution in a fixed time interval, $T = \dfrac{2\pi r}{v}$. Since the motion repeats itself after equal intervals of time, it is periodic.

The motion is not oscillatory because the particle does not move to and fro about a fixed point. Consequently, it is neither simple harmonic nor angular simple harmonic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a simple harmonic motion, which of the following quantities is always positive?
questions.solution:
In simple harmonic motion, the restoring force $\vec{F}$ and acceleration $\vec{a}$ are related by Newton's second law as $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$.

Taking the dot product of $\vec{F}$ and $\vec{a}$, we get:

$\vec{F} \cdot \vec{a} = (m\vec{a}) \cdot \vec{a} = m|\vec{a}|^2 = ma^2$

Since mass $m$ is a positive quantity and the square of the magnitude of acceleration $a^2$ is always non-negative, their product is always positive (or zero at the mean position).

For the other options:

$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{r} = (-\omega^2 \vec{r}) \cdot \vec{r} = -\omega^2 r^2 \le 0$

$\vec{F} \cdot \vec{r} = (-k \vec{r}) \cdot \vec{r} = -k r^2 \le 0$

$\vec{v} \cdot \vec{r}$ can be positive or negative depending on whether the particle is moving away from or towards the mean position.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle travels along a circular path with a continuously increasing speed. Its motion is classified as
questions.solution:
The particle moves along a circular path with continuously increasing speed.

Since the speed is increasing, the time taken to complete each successive revolution decreases. Thus, the motion does not repeat itself in equal intervals of time, meaning it is not periodic.

The motion does not involve moving back and forth about a mean position, so it is not oscillatory.

Therefore, the motion is neither periodic nor oscillatory.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle travels along the X-axis governed by the equation $x = x_0 \sin^2 \omega t$. The motion is simple harmonic
questions.solution:
Given equation of motion is $x = x_0 \sin^2 \omega t$.

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2 \theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$, the equation can be rewritten as:

$x = \dfrac{x_0}{2} (1 - \cos 2\omega t)$

$x - \dfrac{x_0}{2} = - \dfrac{x_0}{2} \cos 2\omega t$

This represents simple harmonic motion about the mean position $x = \dfrac{x_0}{2}$.

The amplitude of the SHM is $A = \dfrac{x_0}{2}$.

The angular frequency of the SHM is $\omega' = 2\omega$.

The time period of the motion is $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega'} = \dfrac{2\pi}{2\omega} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$.
